title: Man and Medicine: Towards Health for All
content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
This chapter traces the historical evolution of medicine and public health from antiquity to the modern era of molecular medicine and global health initiatives. It lays the foundation for understanding how the focus of healthcare shifted from merely treating the sick individual to promoting the health of entire populations.

DEFINITIONS
PUBLIC HEALTH: The science and art of preventing disease, prolonging life, and promoting health and efficiency through organized community effort.
PREVENTIVE MEDICINE: A specialized field of medical practice focusing on the health of individuals and defined populations in order to protect, promote, and maintain health and well-being.
SOCIAL MEDICINE: The study of man as a social being in his total environment, focusing on the social, economic, and cultural causes of disease.
COMMUNITY MEDICINE: The successor to public health and preventive medicine; a specialty dealing with populations that aims to measure the health needs of the community and plan, administer, and evaluate healthcare services.

CORE CONCEPTS
EVOLUTION OF MEDICINE: Medicine has progressed from the organism level to the organ, cell, and molecular levels.
INDIAN MEDICINE: Ayurveda (knowledge of life) relies on the Tridosha theory (Vata, Pitta, Kapha).
GREEK MEDICINE: Introduced the epidemiological approach. Based on the theory of four humors (Phlegm, Yellow Bile, Black Bile, Blood).
DAWN OF SCIENTIFIC MEDICINE: Louis Pasteur demonstrated the germ theory of disease. Robert Koch formulated Koch postulates.
PHASES OF PUBLIC HEALTH:
1. DISEASE CONTROL PHASE (1880 TO 1920): Focused on environmental sanitation and sanitary legislation.
2. HEALTH PROMOTIONAL PHASE (1920 TO 1960): Emphasized maternal and child health and personal hygiene.
3. SOCIAL ENGINEERING PHASE (1960 TO 1980): Highlighted the role of social and behavioral risk factors in chronic diseases.
4. HEALTH FOR ALL PHASE (1981 ONWARDS): Centered on primary healthcare, equity, and community participation as formalized by the Alma-Ata Declaration.

KEY POINTS
CHARAKA is the Father of Indian Medicine.
SUSHRUTA is the Father of Indian Surgery.
HIPPOCRATES is the Father of Medicine and the first true epidemiologist.
ANDREAS VESALIUS is the first man of modern medicine (anatomy).
JOHN SNOW is the Father of Modern Epidemiology for his work on the Broad Street cholera outbreak.

FORMULAS AND CALCULATIONS
NOT APPLICABLE FOR THIS CHAPTER.

MNEMONICS
NOT APPLICABLE FOR THIS CHAPTER.

title: Concept of Health and Disease
content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
This chapter delves into the multifaceted definition of health, its various dimensions, and the complex determinants that shape it. It introduces the fundamental epidemiological models of disease causation and the progressive levels of prevention that form the core of community medicine.


DEFINITIONS
HEALTH (WHO): A state of complete physical, mental and social well-being and not merely the absence of disease or infirmity.
DISEASE: A physiological or psychological dysfunction.
ILLNESS: A subjective state of the person who feels aware of not being well.
EPIDEMIOLOGICAL TRIAD: The standard model of infectious disease causation involving Agent, Host, and Environment.
WEB OF CAUSATION: An epidemiological model used to explain the multifactorial etiology of chronic, non-communicable diseases.

CORE CONCEPTS
DETERMINANTS OF HEALTH: Biological (genetics), behavioral (lifestyle), environmental (internal and external), socio-economic, and health system factors.
INDICATORS OF HEALTH: Used to measure the health status of a community. Includes Mortality, Morbidity, and Disability indicators.
ICEBERG PHENOMENON OF DISEASE: The floating tip represents clinical, diagnosed cases seen by the physician. The vast submerged portion represents hidden, inapparent, presymptomatic, or undiagnosed cases in the community. Screening is used to detect the submerged portion.


LEVELS OF PREVENTION:
1. PRIMORDIAL PREVENTION: Prevention of the development of risk factors in population groups.
2. PRIMARY PREVENTION: Action taken prior to the onset of disease (Health Promotion and Specific Protection).
3. SECONDARY PREVENTION: Action which halts the progress of a disease at its incipient stage (Early Diagnosis and Prompt Treatment).
4. TERTIARY PREVENTION: Measures to reduce or limit impairments and disabilities (Disability Limitation and Rehabilitation).

KEY POINTS
DALY (DISABILITY ADJUSTED LIFE YEAR): 1 DALY equals 1 year of healthy life lost. It measures overall disease burden.
HUMAN DEVELOPMENT INDEX (HDI): Combines Life expectancy at birth, Education index, and GNI per capita. Ranges from 0 to 1.
PHYSICAL QUALITY OF LIFE INDEX (PQLI): Combines Infant mortality, Life expectancy at age one, and Literacy. Ranges from 0 to 100. Does not include per capita income.

FORMULAS AND CALCULATIONS
DALY = YEARS OF LIFE LOST (YLL) + YEARS LIVED WITH DISABILITY (YLD)

MNEMONICS
LEVELS OF PREVENTION MODES OF INTERVENTION:
PRIMORDIAL: No risk factor.
PRIMARY: Risk factor present, no disease.
SECONDARY: Disease present, early stage.
TERTIARY: Advanced disease, preventing disability.

title: Principles of Epidemiology and Epidemiologic Methods
content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
This chapter covers the fundamental principles of epidemiology, the tools used to measure disease frequency, and the specific study designs utilized to investigate associations and establish causation in public health.

DEFINITIONS
EPIDEMIOLOGY: The study of the distribution and determinants of health-related states or events in specified populations, and the application of this study to the control of health problems.
INCIDENCE: The number of NEW cases occurring in a defined population during a specified period of time.
PREVALENCE: All current cases (old and new) existing at a given point in time or over a period of time in a given population.
RATE: Measures the occurrence of an event in a population over time (e.g., Death rate).
RATIO: Expresses a relation in size between two random quantities where the numerator is not necessarily a part of the denominator (e.g., Sex ratio).
PROPORTION: A ratio where the numerator is included in the denominator, usually expressed as a percentage.

CORE CONCEPTS
DESCRIPTIVE EPIDEMIOLOGY: Studies disease distribution by Time, Place, and Person to formulate an etiological hypothesis.
ANALYTICAL EPIDEMIOLOGY:
1. CASE CONTROL STUDY: Proceeds backwards from effect to cause. Both exposure and outcome have occurred before the study starts. Measures Odds Ratio.
2. COHORT STUDY: Proceeds forward from cause to effect. Groups are defined based on exposure to a risk factor. Measures Relative Risk and Attributable Risk.

EXPERIMENTAL EPIDEMIOLOGY: Randomized Controlled Trials (RCT) are the gold standard for testing the efficacy of interventions. Eliminates selection bias through randomization.
ASSOCIATION AND CAUSATION: Evaluated using the Bradford Hill criteria.

KEY POINTS
INCIDENCE is the best measure for acute diseases and for evaluating the effectiveness of preventive measures.
PREVALENCE is useful for chronic diseases and for estimating the administrative and healthcare workload.
CASE CONTROL STUDIES are relatively quick, inexpensive, and ideal for rare diseases.
COHORT STUDIES are time-consuming, expensive, and ideal for rare exposures.
BLINDING in RCTs eliminates bias. Single blind (patient), Double blind (patient and doctor), Triple blind (patient, doctor, and analyzer).

FORMULAS AND CALCULATIONS
INCIDENCE = (NUMBER OF NEW CASES / POPULATION AT RISK) X 1000
PREVALENCE = INCIDENCE X MEAN DURATION OF DISEASE (P = I X D)
ODDS RATIO (CROSS-PRODUCT RATIO) = AD / BC (from a 2x2 contingency table)
RELATIVE RISK = INCIDENCE IN EXPOSED / INCIDENCE IN NON-EXPOSED
ATTRIBUTABLE RISK = ((INCIDENCE IN EXPOSED MINUS INCIDENCE IN NON-EXPOSED) / INCIDENCE IN EXPOSED) X 100

MNEMONICS
BRADFORD HILL CRITERIA FOR CAUSATION (SSC-TBP):
S - STRENGTH OF ASSOCIATION
S - SPECIFICITY
C - CONSISTENCY
T - TEMPORAL RELATIONSHIP (Most important)
B - BIOLOGICAL PLAUSIBILITY
P - POPULATION GRADIENT (Dose-response)

title: Screening for Disease
content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
This chapter explores the principles and applications of screening in public health. It differentiates between screening and diagnostic tests, details the criteria necessary before implementing a screening program, and explains the statistical tools used to evaluate the validity and yield of a screening test.

DEFINITIONS
SCREENING: The active search for unrecognized disease or defect by means of rapidly applied tests, examinations, or other procedures in apparently healthy individuals.
DIAGNOSTIC TEST: A test applied to individuals presenting with clinical symptoms to confirm or rule out a disease.
LEAD TIME: The time interval between disease detection by screening and the time it would have been diagnosed due to the development of symptoms.
LEAD TIME BIAS: The illusion of prolonged survival caused by early detection, when in reality, the natural history of the disease remains unchanged.
LENGTH BIAS: The tendency of screening to detect slowly progressing diseases (which have a longer presymptomatic phase) rather than rapidly fatal ones.

CORE CONCEPTS
ICEBERG PHENOMENON OF DISEASE: The floating tip represents clinical, diagnosed cases. The massive submerged portion represents the hidden mass of disease (latent, presymptomatic, subclinical cases, and carriers). Screening aims to detect this submerged portion.

CRITERIA FOR SCREENING:
1. DISEASE: Should be an important health problem, have a recognizable latent stage, and possess an understood natural history.
2. TEST: Must be acceptable to the population, safe, rapid, inexpensive, valid (sensitive and specific), and reliable.
3. TREATMENT: There must be an accepted and effective treatment for patients with recognized disease, and adequate facilities for diagnosis and treatment must exist.
EVALUATION OF A SCREENING TEST:
A 2x2 contingency table is used comparing the screening test results against a gold standard diagnostic test.

KEY POINTS
Screening is done on apparently healthy people; diagnostic testing is done on sick people.
A highly sensitive test is preferred for screening a fatal disease where missing a case carries a high penalty (e.g., Cervical Cancer).
A highly specific test is preferred when false positives cause severe psychological trauma or when treatment is risky and expensive.
Positive Predictive Value (PPV) is directly proportional to the prevalence of the disease in the population. If prevalence drops, PPV drops, even if the test's sensitivity and specificity remain constant.

FORMULAS AND CALCULATIONS
SENSITIVITY (True Positive Rate) = (TRUE POSITIVES / (TRUE POSITIVES + FALSE NEGATIVES)) X 100
SPECIFICITY (True Negative Rate) = (TRUE NEGATIVES / (TRUE NEGATIVES + FALSE POSITIVES)) X 100
POSITIVE PREDICTIVE VALUE = (TRUE POSITIVES / (TRUE POSITIVES + FALSE POSITIVES)) X 100
NEGATIVE PREDICTIVE VALUE = (TRUE NEGATIVES / (TRUE NEGATIVES + FALSE NEGATIVES)) X 100

MNEMONICS
SN-N-OUT: A test with high SeNsitivity, when Negative, rules OUT the disease.
SP-P-IN: A test with high SPecificity, when Positive, rules IN the disease.

title: Epidemiology of Communicable Diseases
content:
PART I: GENERAL EPIDEMIOLOGY OF COMMUNICABLE DISEASES
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
This chapter covers the dynamics of infectious disease transmission. It details the chain of infection, the various stages of infection, types of carriers, and the critical public health interventions used to break the chain of transmission, including immunization, isolation, and cold chain logistics.


DEFINITIONS
INCUBATION PERIOD: The time interval between the invasion by an infectious agent and the appearance of the first sign or symptom of the disease.
GENERATION TIME: The time interval between the receipt of infection by a host and the maximal infectivity of that host.
SERIAL INTERVAL: The gap in time between the onset of disease in the primary case and the onset of disease in the secondary case.
SECONDARY ATTACK RATE (SAR): The number of exposed persons developing the disease within the range of one incubation period following exposure to a primary case.
ZOONOSIS: An infection or infectious disease transmissible under natural conditions from vertebrate animals to man.

CORE CONCEPTS
DYNAMICS OF DISEASE TRANSMISSION: Transmission requires a Source or Reservoir, a Mode of Transmission, and a Susceptible Host.

CARRIERS: An infected person or animal that harbors a specific infectious agent in the absence of discernible clinical disease but serves as a potential source of infection. 
Types include:
1. INCUBATORY CARRIER: Sheds agent during incubation (e.g., Measles, Mumps).
2. CONVALESCENT CARRIER: Sheds agent during recovery (e.g., Typhoid, Cholera).
3. HEALTHY CARRIER: Sheds agent without ever showing symptoms (e.g., Polio).
COLD CHAIN AND VVM: The logistical system of transporting and storing vaccines at recommended temperatures from manufacturer to the point of use.
- ILR (ICE LINED REFRIGERATOR): Maintains +2 C to +8 C. The most reliable equipment for PHCs due to prolonged cold life during power cuts.
- DEEP FREEZER: Maintains -15 C to -25 C. Used for storing OPV and freezing ice packs.

KEY POINTS
Generation time is often shorter than the incubation period, meaning a person can be highly infectious before they even know they are sick (e.g., COVID-19, Measles).
Typhoid carriers primarily harbor the bacteria in the gallbladder. Fecal carriers are more common and more dangerous than urinary carriers.
Vaccine Vial Monitors (VVM) indicate cumulative heat exposure. If the inner square is lighter than the outer circle, the vaccine is usable. If it matches or is darker, discard it immediately.

FORMULAS AND CALCULATIONS
SECONDARY ATTACK RATE = (NUMBER OF EXPOSED PERSONS DEVELOPING THE DISEASE WITHIN ONE INCUBATION PERIOD / TOTAL NUMBER OF SUSCEPTIBLE CONTACTS) X 100

MNEMONICS
VVM STAGES EVALUATION (L L M D):
L - Lighter (Usable)
L - Lighter, but progressing (Usable)
M - Matches (Discard)
D - Darker (Discard)


--- PART II: RESPIRATORY INFECTIONS ---
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
This chapter focuses on major respiratory droplet-borne infections, detailing their epidemiology, clinical hallmarks, severe complications, and prevention strategies. It emphasizes highly contagious viral diseases like Measles, Rubella, Influenza, and Mumps, as well as bacterial infections like Diphtheria.

DEFINITIONS
DROPLET NUCLEI: Microscopic aerosolized particles (less than 5 microns) containing infectious agents that can remain suspended in the air for long periods and travel long distances.
ANTIGENIC DRIFT: Minor, gradual mutations in the hemagglutinin (HA) or neuraminidase (NA) antigens of the influenza virus, leading to seasonal epidemics.
ANTIGENIC SHIFT: Major, sudden changes in the HA or NA antigens of the influenza A virus (often through genetic reassortment with animal strains), leading to global pandemics.
CONGENITAL RUBELLA SYNDROME (CRS): Fetal anomalies occurring when a pregnant woman is infected with the Rubella virus, especially during the first trimester.

CORE CONCEPTS
MEASLES (RUBEOLA): RNA Paramyxovirus. Highly contagious (SAR > 90%). Koplik spots appear on the buccal mucosa 1-2 days before the maculopapular rash. 
RUBELLA (GERMAN MEASLES): RNA Togavirus. Mild in adults but teratogenic in fetuses. The classic CRS triad consists of Sensorineural Deafness, Cataracts, and Cardiac malformations (primarily Patent Ductus Arteriosus).
INFLUENZA: Orthomyxovirus. Causes rapid outbreaks. Shift vs Drift is a highly tested concept explaining why vaccines must be updated annually.
DIPHTHERIA: Corynebacterium diphtheriae. Characterized by a tough, grayish-white pseudomembrane over the tonsils/pharynx that bleeds upon scraping. Toxin production is evaluated by the Elek test.

KEY POINTS
Otitis media is the most common complication of measles.
Pneumonia is the most common cause of death in measles.
Subacute Sclerosing Panencephalitis (SSPE) is a rare, fatal, late complication of measles.
Administration of Vitamin A (1 lakh to 2 lakh IU) significantly reduces mortality and severity of measles in children.
Mumps commonly causes epididymo-orchitis in post-pubertal males (usually unilateral, rarely leading to complete sterility) and oophoritis in females.
The Schick test historically determined susceptibility to Diphtheria, but is largely obsolete now due to universal immunization.

FORMULAS AND CALCULATIONS
NOT APPLICABLE FOR THIS CHAPTER.

MNEMONICS
CRS TRIAD (D C P):
D - Deafness (Sensorineural)
C - Cataracts
P - Patent Ductus Arteriosus (Cardiac defect)
COMPLICATIONS OF MEASLES (O P S):
O - Otitis media (Most common)
P - Pneumonia (Most fatal)
S - SSPE (Late, fatal neurological)


--- PART III: INTESTINAL INFECTIONS ---
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
This chapter covers major communicable diseases transmitted primarily through the feco-oral route and parenterally. It explores the epidemiology, clinical manifestations, prevention, and public health control strategies for Poliomyelitis, Viral Hepatitis, Cholera, Typhoid, and other food-borne or water-borne pathogens.

DEFINITIONS
VAPP: Vaccine-Associated Paralytic Poliomyelitis. A rare adverse event following the administration of live Oral Poliovirus Vaccine (OPV) where the attenuated virus reverts to neurovirulence.
CVDPV: Circulating Vaccine-Derived Poliovirus. Mutated strains of the OPV virus that regain neurovirulence and transmissibility, causing outbreaks in communities with low immunization coverage.
CARRIER STATE (TYPHOID): An individual who excretes Salmonella typhi in feces or urine for more than one year after the acute illness.
FULMINANT HEPATITIS: A severe, rapidly progressive form of hepatitis resulting in massive hepatic necrosis and liver failure.

CORE CONCEPTS
POLIOMYELITIS: Caused by Poliovirus (Types 1, 2, 3). India is officially certified polio-free. The Polio Endgame Strategy involves the phased withdrawal of OPV (starting with the type 2 component) and the introduction of Inactivated Poliovirus Vaccine (IPV) to prevent VAPP and cVDPV.
VIRAL HEPATITIS:
ENTERICALLY TRANSMITTED: Hepatitis A and E (Feco-oral route). Hepatitis E is particularly dangerous in the third trimester of pregnancy.
PARENTERALLY TRANSMITTED: Hepatitis B, C, and D (Blood, sexual contact, vertical transmission). Hepatitis B virus is a DNA virus, while the others are RNA viruses. Chronic infection is marked by the persistence of HBsAg for more than 6 months. HBeAg positivity indicates high viral replication and maximum infectivity.
CHOLERA: Caused by Vibrio cholerae. Characterized by sudden onset of painless, profuse, watery diarrhea (rice-water stools) and vomiting, leading to severe dehydration and cardiovascular collapse. The mainstay of treatment is prompt rehydration with Oral Rehydration Salts (ORS) or intravenous fluids (Ringer's Lactate).
TYPHOID FEVER: Caused by Salmonella typhi. Characterized by sustained step-ladder fever, relative bradycardia, coated tongue, and rose spots. The gallbladder is the principal reservoir for chronic fecal carriers.

KEY POINTS
HEPATITIS E IN PREGNANCY: Carries a disproportionately high mortality rate (up to 20 percent) due to fulminant hepatic failure.
NEEDLE STICK INJURY RULE OF 3S: The risk of transmission following a percutaneous injury from a known positive source is roughly 30 percent for Hepatitis B, 3 percent for Hepatitis C, and 0.3 percent for HIV.
CHOLERA DIAGNOSIS: The classic rapid diagnostic sign under a microscope is the darting motility of Vibrio cholerae in a hanging drop preparation.
TYPHOID CARRIERS: Fecal carriers are more common and pose a significantly higher public health risk than urinary carriers. Chronic carriage is heavily associated with gallstones.

FORMULAS AND CALCULATIONS
NOT HEAVILY FORMULA-BASED. HOWEVER, ORS OSMOLARITY MUST BE KNOWN: WHO STANDARD LOW OSMOLARITY ORS IS 245 MOSM/L.

MNEMONICS
HEPATITIS TRANSMISSION (VOWELS FOR BOWELS):
HEPATITIS A AND E (THE VOWELS) ARE TRANSMITTED VIA THE BOWELS (FECO-ORAL).
HEPATITIS B, C, AND D ARE TRANSMITTED VIA BLOOD AND BODY FLUIDS.


--- PART IV: ARTHROPOD-BORNE INFECTIONS ---
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
This chapter examines diseases transmitted by arthropod vectors, representing a massive public health burden in India. It details the vector bionomics, transmission cycles, clinical features, and integrated vector management strategies for Malaria, Dengue, Chikungunya, Japanese Encephalitis, and Lymphatic Filariasis.

DEFINITIONS
DEFINITIVE HOST: The host in which the parasite undergoes its sexual cycle of reproduction (e.g., the mosquito in Malaria).
INTERMEDIATE HOST: The host in which the parasite undergoes its asexual cycle of reproduction (e.g., humans in Malaria).
EXTRINSIC INCUBATION PERIOD: The time required for the development of the disease agent in the vector, from the time of blood meal to the time the vector becomes infective.
BIOLOGICAL TRANSMISSION: Occurs when the agent multiplies or undergoes developmental changes within the vector. Types include Propagative (Plague bacilli in flea), Cyclo-developmental (Filarial parasite in mosquito), and Cyclo-propagative (Malaria parasite in mosquito).

CORE CONCEPTS
MALARIA: Transmitted by female Anopheles mosquitoes. Plasmodium falciparum is the most dangerous species, causing severe/cerebral malaria due to its ability to cause sequestration of infected red blood cells in deep microvasculature. Radical cure for P. vivax and P. ovale requires 14 days of Primaquine to eradicate liver hypnozoites and prevent relapse.
DENGUE: Transmitted by Aedes aegypti (a day-biting mosquito that breeds in clean, stagnant artificial water containers). Dengue Hemorrhagic Fever (DHF) is characterized by increased vascular permeability, leading to plasma leakage, hemoconcentration (rising hematocrit), and thrombocytopenia.
CHIKUNGUNYA: Also transmitted by Aedes mosquitoes. Clinically distinguished from Dengue by the presence of severe, prolonged, and debilitating polyarthralgia (joint pain) that can persist for months.
JAPANESE ENCEPHALITIS (JE): Transmitted by Culex tritaeniorhynchus, which breeds in flooded rice paddies. The transmission cycle involves Ardeid birds as natural reservoirs and pigs as amplifying hosts. Humans are dead-end hosts because the viremia is insufficient to infect feeding mosquitoes.
LYMPHATIC FILARIASIS: Caused predominantly by Wuchereria bancrofti and transmitted by Culex quinquefasciatus. Characterized by nocturnal periodicity of microfilariae in the peripheral blood. Control relies heavily on Mass Drug Administration (MDA).

KEY POINTS
ANOPHELES MOSQUITO VECTORS IN INDIA: Anopheles culicifacies (rural/plains), Anopheles stephensi (urban), Anopheles fluviatilis (hills/forests).
WARNING SIGNS IN DENGUE: Severe abdominal pain, persistent vomiting, mucosal bleeding, lethargy, hepatomegaly, and a rising hematocrit with a rapid drop in platelet count.
TOURNIQUET TEST: A clinical test used in Dengue to assess capillary fragility. It is considered positive if 10 or more petechiae per square inch are observed after inflating a blood pressure cuff midway between systolic and diastolic pressure for 5 minutes.
PIGS IN JE: Pigs act as the ultimate amplifying host for the JE virus. The virus multiplies exponentially in the pig without causing any clinical disease to the animal itself.

FORMULAS AND CALCULATIONS
ANNUAL PARASITE INCIDENCE (API) = (CONFIRMED CASES DURING 1 YEAR / POPULATION UNDER SURVEILLANCE) X 1000
ANNUAL BLOOD EXAMINATION RATE (ABER) = (NUMBER OF BLOOD SMEARS EXAMINED IN A YEAR / TOTAL POPULATION) X 100

MNEMONICS
TYPES OF BIOLOGICAL TRANSMISSION (P C CP):
PROPAGATIVE: PLAGUE (Multiplies only)
CYCLO-DEVELOPMENTAL: FILARIASIS (Changes stage only, no multiplication)
CYCLO-PROPAGATIVE: MALARIA (Multiplies AND changes stage)


--- PART V: ZOONOSES AND SURFACE INFECTIONS ---
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
This chapter covers infectious diseases naturally transmitted between vertebrate animals and humans (Zoonoses), as well as diseases acquired through contact with contaminated soil or surfaces. It provides an exhaustive look at Rabies, Brucellosis, Plague, Tetanus, and Leprosy, focusing on highly tested post-exposure prophylaxis guidelines.

DEFINITIONS
ZOONOSES: Infections or infectious diseases transmissible under natural conditions from vertebrate animals to man.
ANTHROPOZOONOSES: Infections transmitted to man from lower vertebrate animals (e.g., Rabies).
ZOOANTHROPONOSES: Infections transmitted from man to lower vertebrate animals (e.g., Human tuberculosis to cattle).
AMPHIXENOSIS: Infections maintained in both man and lower vertebrate animals that may be transmitted in either direction (e.g., T. cruzi).
EPIZOOTIC: An outbreak (epidemic) of disease in an animal population.

CORE CONCEPTS
RABIES: A 100 percent fatal viral encephalitis caused by a Lyssavirus. Diagnosis is confirmed post-mortem by the presence of Negri bodies in the brain. Public health management strictly depends on bite categorization and Post-Exposure Prophylaxis (PEP). The latest guidelines mandate that all Rabies Immunoglobulin (RIG) be infiltrated entirely locally into and around the wound; no remaining RIG should be given intramuscularly at a distant site.
BRUCELLOSIS (MALTA FEVER): An occupational zoonosis affecting dairy workers, veterinarians, and abattoir workers. Transmitted via raw milk or direct contact with infected animal tissues. Characterized by undulant (wave-like) fever, severe night sweats, and polyarthritis.
PLAGUE: Caused by Yersinia pestis, showing characteristic bipolar safety pin staining. Maintained in rat populations and transmitted by the rat flea (Xenopsylla cheopis). The bubonic form causes severely enlarged, painful lymph nodes (buboes). The pneumonic form is highly fatal and transmits directly from human to human via droplets.
TETANUS: Caused by the exotoxin (tetanospasmin) of Clostridium tetani, which spores survive in soil. Causes severe muscle spasms, trismus (lockjaw), and opisthotonos. Neonatal tetanus results from unhygienic cutting of the umbilical cord and lack of maternal immunization.
LEPROSY (HANSEN'S DISEASE): Caused by Mycobacterium leprae. It primarily affects the skin and peripheral nerves, causing anesthesia, muscle weakness, and deformities. India achieved elimination as a public health problem (<1 case per 10000 population) at the national level in 2005.

KEY POINTS
RABIES BITE CATEGORIES:
CATEGORY I: Touching or feeding animals, licks on intact skin. NO VACCINE REQUIRED. Just wash the exposed skin.
CATEGORY II: Nibbling of uncovered skin, minor scratches or abrasions without bleeding. REQUIRES VACCINE and immediate wound washing.
CATEGORY III: Single or multiple transdermal bites or scratches, licks on broken skin, contamination of mucous membrane with saliva, and ALL BAT EXPOSURES. REQUIRES VACCINE PLUS RABIES IMMUNOGLOBULIN (RIG) and immediate wound washing.
INTRADERMAL RABIES VACCINATION (THAI RED CROSS REGIMEN): Consists of 0.1 ml injected intradermally at two distinct sites (deltoids) on days 0, 3, 7, and 28. This dose-sparing technique is highly cost-effective.
TETANUS NEONATORUM ELIMINATION: Defined as less than 1 case of neonatal tetanus per 1000 live births in every single district of the country. India successfully achieved maternal and neonatal tetanus elimination (MNTE) in 2015.

FORMULAS AND CALCULATIONS
NOT APPLICABLE FOR THIS CHAPTER.

MNEMONICS
RABIES BITE CATEGORY MANAGEMENT (W V R):
CATEGORY I: WASH ONLY.
CATEGORY II: WASH + VACCINE.
CATEGORY III: WASH + VACCINE + RIG (RABIES IMMUNOGLOBULIN).

title: Epidemiology of Chronic Non-Communicable Diseases and Conditions
content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
This chapter addresses the growing global and national burden of Non-Communicable Diseases (NCDs). It explores the multifactorial etiology of chronic conditions like Cardiovascular Diseases, Hypertension, Diabetes Mellitus, Obesity, Cancer, and Blindness, emphasizing the shift from infectious to lifestyle-related diseases.

DEFINITIONS
NON-COMMUNICABLE DISEASE: A medical condition or disease that is non-infectious, typically of long duration and slow progression.
RISK FACTOR: An attribute, characteristic, or exposure of an individual that increases the likelihood of developing a disease or injury.
TRACKING OF BLOOD PRESSURE: The phenomenon where individuals whose blood pressure initially tracks in a higher percentile continue to remain in higher percentiles as they age.
THE RULE OF HALVES: A historical epidemiological concept stating that half the hypertensive population is undiagnosed, half of those diagnosed are untreated, and half of those treated are inadequately treated.

CORE CONCEPTS
WEB OF CAUSATION: Unlike communicable diseases, NCDs lack a single specific agent. They are caused by complex interactions of non-modifiable factors (age, sex, genetics) and modifiable factors (tobacco, alcohol, physical inactivity, unhealthy diet).

WHO STEPWISE APPROACH TO SURVEILLANCE:
STEP 1: Questionnaire to gather demographic and behavioral risk factor data.
STEP 2: Physical measurements (blood pressure, height, weight, waist circumference).
STEP 3: Biochemical measurements (fasting blood glucose and total cholesterol).
CORONARY HEART DISEASE (CHD): Primary prevention involves a Prudent Diet where total dietary fat is limited to 20 to 30 percent of daily energy, saturated fats to <7 percent, and dietary cholesterol to <200 mg/day.
DIABETES MELLITUS DIAGNOSTIC CRITERIA (ADA/WHO):
1. Fasting Plasma Glucose >= 126 mg/dL.
2. 2-hour Post-Prandial Glucose >= 200 mg/dL.
3. HbA1c >= 6.5 percent.
4. Random plasma glucose >= 200 mg/dL with classic symptoms of hyperglycemia.
OBESITY: Assessed via Body Mass Index. Asian Indian consensus guidelines suggest lower cutoffs compared to WHO: Normal 18.0 to 22.9, Overweight 23.0 to 24.9, Obese >= 25.0.

CANCER: The most common cancers in Indian males are Oral cavity and Lung; in females, Breast and Cervix. Visual Inspection with Acetic Acid (VIA) is the recommended low-cost screening tool for cervical cancer in resource-limited settings.
BLINDNESS: Defined in India as visual acuity of less than 3/60 or inability to count fingers at 3 meters in the better eye with best possible correction. Cataract remains the absolute leading cause.

KEY POINTS
The four major behavioral risk factors for NCDs are tobacco use, unhealthy diet, physical inactivity, and harmful use of alcohol.
India is experiencing a double burden of disease (simultaneous high rates of both communicable and non-communicable diseases).

FORMULAS AND CALCULATIONS
BODY MASS INDEX (QUETELET INDEX) = WEIGHT (IN KG) / (HEIGHT IN METERS SQUARED)

MNEMONICS
MODIFIABLE RISK FACTORS FOR NCDS (T A D P):
T - TOBACCO USE
A - ALCOHOL (HARMFUL USE)
D - DIET (UNHEALTHY)
P - PHYSICAL INACTIVITY

title: Health Programmes in India
description: Comprehensive strategies and interventions launched by the Government of India to control and eradicate major diseases.

subsections.title: National Tuberculosis Elimination Programme (NTEP)
subsections.content:
OVERVIEW AND GOALS
Previously known as the Revised National Tuberculosis Control Programme (RNTCP). The NTEP represents India's aggressive, modernized approach to combating the TB epidemic, shifting from merely controlling the disease to complete elimination.

TARGETS AND TIMELINES
To eliminate TB in India by 2025. This ambitious target is set 5 years ahead of the global Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) target of 2030.

KEY STRATEGIES AND INTERVENTIONS
THE DTPB STRATEGY:
- DETECT: Active case finding and Universal Drug Susceptibility Testing (UDST) utilizing rapid molecular diagnostics like CBNAAT and Truenat upfront for all suspected cases.
- TREAT: Daily regimen DOTS with fixed-dose combinations. For MDR/RR-TB, India has rolled out the shorter, highly effective, all-oral BPaLM regimen.
- PREVENT: Airborne infection control, active contact tracing, and TB Preventive Treatment (TPT) using 3HP or 1HP regimens for high-risk contacts.
- BUILD: Health system strengthening and multisectoral coordination.

IMPORTANT UPDATES AND RECENT CHANGES
BPALM REGIMEN: The introduction of the 6-month, all-oral BPaLM (Bedaquiline, Pretomanid, Linezolid, Moxifloxacin) regimen for MDR-TB represents a monumental shift away from longer, toxic, injectable-based regimens.
NI-KSHAY POSHAN YOJANA: A Direct Benefit Transfer (DBT) scheme providing Rs 500 per month to all notified TB patients to support nutritional needs during treatment.
PRADHAN MANTRI TB MUKT BHARAT ABHIYAAN: A community engagement initiative that allows individuals and organizations (Ni-kshay Mitras) to adopt TB patients and provide additional nutritional and vocational support.

HIGH-YIELD FACTS AND MNEMONICS
DTPB STRATEGY:
D - DETECT
T - TREAT
P - PREVENT
B - BUILD

subsections.title: National Center for Vector Borne Diseases Control (NCVBDC)
subsections.content:
OVERVIEW AND GOALS
Previously known as NVBDCP. This umbrella program covers the surveillance, prevention, and control of six major vector-borne diseases in India: Malaria, Dengue, Chikungunya, Kala-azar, Japanese Encephalitis (JE), and Lymphatic Filariasis.

TARGETS AND TIMELINES
MALARIA: Target for national elimination by 2030 (achieving an Annual Parasite Incidence of < 1 per 1000 population).
KALA-AZAR: Elimination target is <1 case per 10,000 population at the block level.
LYMPHATIC FILARIASIS: Targeted for global elimination.

KEY STRATEGIES AND INTERVENTIONS
MALARIA: Early diagnosis using Rapid Diagnostic Tests (RDTs). Complete treatment utilizing Artemisinin-based Combination Therapy (ACT) for P. falciparum. Integrated Vector Management relies heavily on Indoor Residual Spraying (IRS) and the distribution of Long-Lasting Insecticidal Nets (LLINs).
LYMPHATIC FILARIASIS: Operates on a twin-pillar strategy:
1. Mass Drug Administration (MDA): Utilizing the highly effective IDA triple-drug therapy.
2. Morbidity Management and Disability Prevention (MMDP): Providing hydrocelectomy surgeries and home-based foot care kits.

IMPORTANT UPDATES AND RECENT CHANGES
IDA STRATEGY: The shift to the triple-drug IDA regimen (Ivermectin, Diethylcarbamazine, Albendazole) has drastically accelerated the clearance of microfilariae from the blood in endemic populations compared to the older two-drug regimen.

HIGH-YIELD FACTS AND MNEMONICS
IDA REGIMEN FOR FILARIASIS:
I - IVERMECTIN
D - DIETHYLCARBAMAZINE (DEC)
A - ALBENDAZOLE

subsections.title: National Leprosy Eradication Programme (NLEP)
subsections.content:
OVERVIEW AND GOALS
One of the oldest health programs in India, aimed at detecting and treating leprosy to halt transmission and prevent irreversible nerve damage and disabilities.

TARGETS AND TIMELINES
GOAL: Achieving Zero Leprosy (Zero infection, Zero disability, and Zero stigma).
India achieved the national elimination target (<1 case per 10,000 population) in 2005. The current focus is strictly on sub-national elimination at the district and block levels.

KEY STRATEGIES AND INTERVENTIONS
ACTIVE CASE DETECTION AND REGULAR SURVEILLANCE (ACDRS): Deploying health workers for house-to-house screening in highly endemic pockets.
MULTI-DRUG THERAPY (MDT): Provided entirely free of cost. Consists of Rifampicin, Dapsone, and Clofazimine.
DISABILITY PREVENTION AND MEDICAL REHABILITATION (DPMR): Provision of custom Micro-Cellular Rubber (MCR) footwear and reconstructive surgeries for established deformities.

IMPORTANT UPDATES AND RECENT CHANGES
SDR-PEP (SINGLE DOSE RIFAMPICIN POST-EXPOSURE PROPHYLAXIS): A major recent update where a single prophylactic dose of Rifampicin is administered to all eligible close contacts of a newly diagnosed leprosy patient to break the chain of transmission.
SPARSH LEPROSY AWARENESS CAMPAIGNS (SLAC): Aggressive national campaigns aimed at dismantling the deep-rooted social stigma surrounding the disease.

HIGH-YIELD FACTS AND MNEMONICS
ZERO LEPROSY TRIAD:
1. ZERO INFECTION
2. ZERO DISABILITY
3. ZERO STIGMA

subsections.title: National AIDS and STD Control Programme (NACP)
subsections.content:
OVERVIEW AND GOALS
A comprehensive program aimed at preventing new HIV infections, providing care and support to people living with HIV/AIDS (PLHIV), and managing Sexually Transmitted Diseases (STDs).

TARGETS AND TIMELINES
PHASE V (2021-2026) aims to achieve the UNAIDS 95-95-95 targets by 2025. It also targets an 80 percent reduction in annual new HIV infections and AIDS-related mortalities, alongside the complete elimination of vertical transmission of HIV and Syphilis.

KEY STRATEGIES AND INTERVENTIONS
TARGETED INTERVENTIONS (TI): Specialized outreach, peer education, and condom promotion specifically for High-Risk Groups (HRGs) such as female sex workers, men who have sex with men, and injecting drug users.
ANTIRETROVIRAL THERAPY (ART): Provision of free, life-long ART to all individuals testing positive, regardless of their CD4 count (the Treat All policy).

IMPORTANT UPDATES AND RECENT CHANGES
DOLUTEGRAVIR TRANSITION: The national program has heavily transitioned to Dolutegravir-based regimens as the first-line ART due to its high genetic barrier to resistance and rapid viral suppression capabilities.
SAMPOORNA SURAKSHA KENDRAS: Newly established centers designed to provide a comprehensive, single-window package of services for individuals at high risk of HIV and STDs.

HIGH-YIELD FACTS AND MNEMONICS
95-95-95 TARGETS:
95 PERCENT of people living with HIV know their status.
95 PERCENT of those diagnosed are on sustained Antiretroviral Therapy.
95 PERCENT of those on ART achieve viral suppression.

subsections.title: National Programme for Prevention and Control of Non-Communicable Diseases (NP-NCD)
subsections.content:
OVERVIEW AND GOALS
Previously known as NPCDCS, this program was restructured and renamed as NP-NCD to broaden its scope beyond just cardiovascular diseases, diabetes, and cancers. It aims to prevent and control the massive burden of chronic, lifestyle-related diseases in India through health promotion and early diagnosis.

TARGETS AND TIMELINES
75/25 INITIATIVE: A highly ambitious target launched to ensure that 75 million people with Hypertension and Diabetes are diagnosed and put on standard care by the year 2025.

KEY STRATEGIES AND INTERVENTIONS
POPULATION BASED SCREENING (PBS): Comprehensive screening for everyone aged 30 years and above for Hypertension, Diabetes, and three common cancers (Oral, Breast, Cervical). This is actively carried out by ASHAs and Community Health Officers (CHOs) at Ayushman Arogya Mandirs.
NCD CLINICS: Established at Community Health Centres (CHCs) and District Hospitals for comprehensive management and referral.

IMPORTANT UPDATES AND RECENT CHANGES
PROGRAM EXPANSION: The program has officially expanded to include the screening and management of Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD), Asthma, Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD), and Non-Alcoholic Fatty Liver Disease (NAFLD).
STEMI MANAGEMENT: Incorporation of the ST-Elevation Myocardial Infarction (STEMI) management protocol to reduce mortality from acute heart attacks by ensuring rapid thrombolysis at peripheral centers.

HIGH-YIELD FACTS AND MNEMONICS
COMMON CANCERS SCREENED IN NP-NCD (O B C):
O - ORAL CANCER
B - BREAST CANCER
C - CERVICAL CANCER

subsections.title: National Programme for Control of Blindness and Visual Impairment (NPCBVI)
subsections.content:
OVERVIEW AND GOALS
Launched as a 100 percent centrally sponsored scheme, NPCBVI is dedicated to reducing the massive burden of avoidable blindness in India, with a heavy emphasis on clearing the backlog of untreated cataracts.

TARGETS AND TIMELINES
The ultimate target is to reduce the prevalence of blindness in India to 0.25 percent.

KEY STRATEGIES AND INTERVENTIONS
CATARACT SURGERY: The cornerstone of the program is the provision of free cataract surgeries utilizing modern Intraocular Lens (IOL) implantation, heavily supported by NGOs and private practitioners.
SCHOOL EYE SCREENING: Active screening of school children for refractive errors and provision of free spectacles.
EYE BANKING: Promotion of eye donation campaigns and strengthening of eye bank infrastructure for corneal transplantation.

IMPORTANT UPDATES AND RECENT CHANGES
DEFINITION UPDATE: India officially updated its definition of blindness to match the WHO criteria. Blindness is now defined as presenting visual acuity of less than 3/60 (Snellen) or inability to count fingers at a distance of 3 meters in the better eye with available correction. (The older Indian definition was < 6/60).

HIGH-YIELD FACTS AND MNEMONICS
CATARACT accounts for more than 60 percent of all blindness in India, making it the most critical target for surgical intervention under NPCBVI.

subsections.title: National Mental Health Programme (NMHP) and Tele-MANAS
subsections.content:
OVERVIEW AND GOALS
NMHP aims to ensure the availability and accessibility of minimum mental healthcare for all, particularly focusing on the most vulnerable and underprivileged sections of the population, while actively fighting the stigma attached to mental illness.

TARGETS AND TIMELINES
To provide community-based mental health services and to reduce the suicide mortality rate significantly by increasing access to counseling and psychiatric interventions.

KEY STRATEGIES AND INTERVENTIONS
DISTRICT MENTAL HEALTH PROGRAMME (DMHP): The operational arm of NMHP. It integrates mental health care with primary health care. It provides psychiatric OPDs at district hospitals, free psychotropic drugs, and community awareness campaigns.
THE BELLARY MODEL: The DMHP is based on the highly successful Bellary Model of community psychiatry.

IMPORTANT UPDATES AND RECENT CHANGES
TELE-MANAS: The National Tele-Mental Health Programme (Tele-MANAS) was launched to provide free, 24/7 tele-mental health services across the country. It serves as a vital digital arm, utilizing toll-free numbers to connect distressed individuals with trained counselors and psychiatrists immediately.

HIGH-YIELD FACTS AND MNEMONICS
DMHP COMPONENTS (T A P E):
T - TREATMENT (Psychiatric drugs at PHC/CHC)
A - AWARENESS (Community campaigns)
P - PREVENTION (Suicide prevention)
E - EDUCATION (Training primary care doctors)

subsections.title: Universal Immunization Programme (UIP) and Mission Indradhanush
subsections.content:
OVERVIEW AND GOALS
One of the largest public health interventions in the world, the UIP provides life-saving vaccines to millions of infants and pregnant women annually, entirely free of cost.

TARGETS AND TIMELINES
The core target is to achieve and sustain Full Immunization Coverage (FIC) of over 90 percent across all districts in India.

KEY STRATEGIES AND INTERVENTIONS
ROUTINE IMMUNIZATION: Protects against 12 vaccine-preventable diseases nationally (Diphtheria, Pertussis, Tetanus, Polio, Measles, Rubella, severe form of Childhood Tuberculosis, Hepatitis B, Hemophilus influenzae type b, Pneumonia/Meningitis due to Pneumococcus, and Rotavirus diarrhea), plus Japanese Encephalitis in endemic districts.
MISSION INDRADHANUSH: A massive, periodic catch-up campaign specifically targeting high-risk, hard-to-reach, and underserved areas to vaccinate children and pregnant women who missed their routine doses.

IMPORTANT UPDATES AND RECENT CHANGES
U-WIN PLATFORM: Rolled out nationwide, U-WIN is the digital registry for routine immunization (modeled after Co-WIN). It tracks every pregnant woman and child, sends SMS reminders, and generates digital vaccination certificates.
OPEN VIAL POLICY: Applicable to specific vaccines (like OPV, Hepatitis B, DPT, TT, Pentavalent) allowing them to be used for up to 28 days after opening, drastically reducing vaccine wastage.

HIGH-YIELD FACTS AND MNEMONICS
VACCINES NOT COVERED BY OPEN VIAL POLICY (MEASLES, BCG, JE, ROTAVIRUS):
These are reconstituted live vaccines (except Rotavirus) and must be discarded within 4 hours of reconstitution to prevent toxic shock syndrome.

subsections.title: Reproductive, Maternal, Newborn, Child, Adolescent Health and Nutrition (RMNCAH+N)
subsections.content:
OVERVIEW AND GOALS
A comprehensive life-cycle approach that recognizes that maternal and child health cannot be addressed in isolation. It integrates interventions from pre-pregnancy through adolescence to tackle maternal mortality, infant mortality, and malnutrition.

TARGETS AND TIMELINES
To reduce the Maternal Mortality Ratio (MMR) to less than 70 per 100,000 live births and the Infant Mortality Rate (IMR) to single digits, aligning with the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs).

KEY STRATEGIES AND INTERVENTIONS
MATERNAL: 
- JANANI SURAKSHA YOJANA (JSY): Conditional cash transfer to promote institutional deliveries.
- PRADHAN MANTRI SURAKSHIT MATRITVA ABHIYAN (PMSMA): Assured, free, and quality ANC provided by a Medical Officer strictly on the 9th of every month.
NEWBORN AND CHILD:
- JANANI SHISHU SURAKSHA KARYAKRAM (JSSK): Entitles all pregnant women delivering in public health institutions to absolutely free and no-expense delivery, including C-sections, free drugs, and free transport.
- RASHTRIYA BAL SWASTHYA KARYAKRAM (RBSK): Systematic screening of children from birth to 18 years for the 4Ds.
ADOLESCENT:
- RASHTRIYA KISHOR SWASTHYA KARYAKRAM (RKSK): Focuses on peer educators, adolescent-friendly health clinics, and Weekly Iron Folic Acid Supplementation (WIFS).

IMPORTANT UPDATES AND RECENT CHANGES
SUMAN (SURAKSHIT MATRITVA AASHWASAN): A massive initiative launched to achieve zero preventable maternal and newborn deaths by providing assured, dignified, and free healthcare with zero tolerance for denial of services.
SWASTH NARI ABHIYAAN: A recent push for comprehensive women-centric care, integrating anemia testing, cervical cancer screening, and maternal health directly at the community level.

HIGH-YIELD FACTS AND MNEMONICS
THE 4Ds OF RBSK:
1. DEFECTS AT BIRTH
2. DISEASES
3. DEFICIENCIES
4. DEVELOPMENTAL DELAYS (INCLUDING DISABILITIES)

subsections.title: National Viral Hepatitis Control Program (NVHCP)
subsections.content:
OVERVIEW AND GOALS
A highly specialized program integrated within the National Health Mission to combat the massive, often silent epidemic of viral hepatitis, which leads to cirrhosis and hepatocellular carcinoma.

TARGETS AND TIMELINES
The ultimate target is to eliminate viral hepatitis as a public health threat by the year 2030, in strict alignment with Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) 3.3.

KEY STRATEGIES AND INTERVENTIONS
PREVENTIVE: Universal immunization against Hepatitis B at birth and subsequent doses.
DIAGNOSTIC AND CURATIVE: Provision of free screening, diagnosis, and highly effective treatment at designated Model Treatment Centres (MTCs) and Treatment Centres (TCs) across district hospitals.

IMPORTANT UPDATES AND RECENT CHANGES
FREE DIRECT ACTING ANTIVIRALS (DAAs): The program's biggest game-changer is the provision of free oral DAAs (like Sofosbuvir and Daclatasvir) for Hepatitis C. Unlike Hepatitis B which is only suppressed, Hepatitis C is now completely curable with a 12 to 24-week course of these drugs.

HIGH-YIELD FACTS AND MNEMONICS
HEPATITIS PROGRESSION (B S, C C):
HEPATITIS B is SUPPRESSED (requires lifelong medication often).
HEPATITIS C is CURABLE (via Direct Acting Antivirals).

subsections.title: National Rabies Control Programme (NRCP)
subsections.content:
OVERVIEW AND GOALS
A vital program dealing with a disease that has a 100 percent mortality rate but is 100 percent preventable. It focuses on both human post-exposure prophylaxis and animal population control.

TARGETS AND TIMELINES
ZERO BY 30: The global and national target to completely eradicate dog-mediated human rabies deaths by the year 2030.

KEY STRATEGIES AND INTERVENTIONS
HUMAN COMPONENT: Ensuring the uninterrupted availability of free Anti-Rabies Vaccine (ARV) and Rabies Immunoglobulin (RIG) at all public health facilities. Promotion of the dose-sparing Intradermal (ID) route for vaccination.
ANIMAL COMPONENT: Inter-sectoral coordination with the Animal Husbandry department for the Animal Birth Control (ABC) program and mass dog vaccination.

IMPORTANT UPDATES AND RECENT CHANGES
RIG INFILTRATION UPDATE: The WHO and national guidelines have strictly updated the protocol for Rabies Immunoglobulin. RIG must be infiltrated entirely into and around the bite wounds. Any mathematically remaining RIG should NOT be injected intramuscularly at a distant site, as was done in the past, because it provides no added benefit and wastes expensive resources.

HIGH-YIELD FACTS AND MNEMONICS
THAI RED CROSS INTRADERMAL SCHEDULE (2-2-2-0-2):
Administered as 2 doses (0.1 ml each) on Days 0, 3, 7, and 28.

subsections.title: Ayushman Bharat and Healthcare Infrastructure
subsections.content:
OVERVIEW AND GOALS
Ayushman Bharat is the flagship scheme of the Government of India, representing a paradigm shift from a sectoral, segmented approach to a comprehensive, need-based health care service. It forms the backbone of India's march towards Universal Health Coverage (UHC).

TARGETS AND TIMELINES
To drastically reduce catastrophic out-of-pocket expenditure (OOPE) on healthcare and provide comprehensive primary, secondary, and tertiary care to the bottom 40 percent of the population.

KEY STRATEGIES AND INTERVENTIONS
PILLAR 1: AYUSHMAN AROGYA MANDIRS (FORMERLY HWCs):
Over 1.72 lakh Sub-centres and Primary Health Centres have been upgraded to provide Comprehensive Primary Health Care (CPHC), moving beyond maternal and child health to include NCD screening, ophthalmic, ENT, and basic oral healthcare.
PILLAR 2: PRADHAN MANTRI JAN AROGYA YOJANA (PM-JAY):
The world's largest publicly funded health insurance scheme. It provides a health cover of Rs. 5 lakhs per family per year for secondary and tertiary care hospitalization, covering over 12 crore poor and vulnerable families.

IMPORTANT UPDATES AND RECENT CHANGES
SENIOR CITIZEN EXPANSION: A massive recent update expanded PM-JAY coverage to all citizens aged 70 years and above, irrespective of their income or socioeconomic status.
ASHA AND ANGANWADI INCLUSION: The insurance cover has also been extended to all frontline healthcare workers, including ASHAs, Anganwadi Workers, and Anganwadi Helpers.
AYUSHMAN BHARAT DIGITAL MISSION (ABDM): The creation of the Ayushman Bharat Health Account (ABHA) ID, a 14-digit unique health identifier to link patient records across hospitals and clinics nationally.

HIGH-YIELD FACTS AND MNEMONICS
PM-JAY ENTITLEMENT:
Rs. 5 LAKHS per FAMILY per YEAR (There is no cap on family size or age of members).

subsections.title: Specialized Target Programmes (NIDDCP, NPPCF, PMNDP)
subsections.content:
OVERVIEW AND GOALS
This module covers highly targeted national programs addressing specific micronutrient deficiencies (Iodine and Fluorine) and critical tertiary care support for end-stage renal disease.

TARGETS AND TIMELINES
NIDDCP (IODINE): To bring the prevalence of Iodine Deficiency Disorders below 5 percent in the entire country and ensure 100 percent consumption of adequately iodized salt.
NPPCF (FLUOROSIS): To prevent and control fluorosis in endemic districts.
PMNDP (DIALYSIS): To provide free dialysis services to all Below Poverty Line (BPL) patients at district hospitals.

KEY STRATEGIES AND INTERVENTIONS
NIDDCP: The core strategy is the universal iodization of edible salt. Routine surveys are conducted to estimate goiter prevalence and test the iodine content in salt at the household level.
NPPCF: Focuses on alternate safe water sources, defluoridation of water, and nutritional interventions (promoting diets rich in calcium and vitamin C to mitigate fluorosis).

PMNDP: Rolled out under the Public-Private Partnership (PPP) mode at district hospitals to reduce the massive out-of-pocket expenditure associated with chronic kidney disease.

IMPORTANT UPDATES AND RECENT CHANGES
IODINE LEVELS: The statutory requirement for iodine in salt is 30 ppm at the manufacturing level and 15 ppm at the consumer level to account for transit losses.

HIGH-YIELD FACTS AND MNEMONICS
ENDEMICITY CRITERIA FOR IDD:
A district is declared endemic for IDD if the prevalence of goiter in children aged 6 to 12 years is greater than 5 percent, or if the median urinary iodine excretion is less than 100 mcg/L.

subsections.title: Targeted Care Programmes (NPHCE, NPPCD, NPPMBI, NOHP)
subsections.content:
OVERVIEW AND GOALS
This section consolidates programs dedicated to specialized life-stage care and specific morbidities, including geriatric health, deafness, burn injuries, and oral health, which are increasingly contributing to the DALY burden in India.

TARGETS AND TIMELINES
NPHCE (ELDERLY CARE): To provide dedicated, accessible, and comprehensive health care to the aging population (60+ years) at all levels of the health care delivery system.
NPPCD (DEAFNESS): To prevent avoidable hearing loss and ensure early identification, diagnosis, and treatment of ear problems.

KEY STRATEGIES AND INTERVENTIONS
NPHCE: Establishment of dedicated Geriatric OPDs and wards (usually 10-bedded) at District Hospitals, and setting up Regional Geriatric Centres at designated medical colleges.
NPPCD: Capacity building of medical officers and grassroots workers to screen for hearing impairment. Provision of hearing aids to the needy.

NPPMBI (BURN INJURIES): Establishing dedicated burn units in government medical colleges and district hospitals, alongside aggressive awareness campaigns on domestic safety.
NOHP (ORAL HEALTH): Integration of oral health care with standard primary health care, utilizing the e-DantSeva portal for widespread awareness and facility mapping.

IMPORTANT UPDATES AND RECENT CHANGES
AYUSHMAN AROGYA MANDIRS: Both NPHCE and NOHP have been heavily integrated into the Comprehensive Primary Health Care (CPHC) packages delivered at the upgraded Ayushman Arogya Mandirs, shifting the focus from tertiary care back to the community level.

HIGH-YIELD FACTS AND MNEMONICS
NPHCE TARGET POPULATION:
Strictly targets individuals aged 60 years and above for dedicated geriatric services.

title: Demography and Family Planning
content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
This chapter examines population dynamics, vital statistics, and the demographic transition of India. It details the various methods of contraception, their mechanisms of action, efficacy, and the concept of eligible and target couples in the context of the National Family Welfare Programme.

DEFINITIONS
DEMOGRAPHY: The scientific study of human populations, primarily with respect to their size, structure, and development.
ELIGIBLE COUPLE: A currently married couple wherein the wife is in the reproductive age group (15 to 45 years).
TOTAL FERTILITY RATE (TFR): The average number of children a woman would have if she were to pass through her reproductive years bearing children at the same rates as the women now in each age group.
NET REPRODUCTION RATE (NRR): The number of daughters a newborn girl will bear during her lifetime assuming fixed age-specific fertility and mortality rates.

CORE CONCEPTS
DEMOGRAPHIC CYCLE:
FIRST STAGE (HIGH STATIONARY): High birth and death rates. Stationary population.
SECOND STAGE (EARLY EXPANDING): Declining death rate, unchanged birth rate. Rapid growth.
THIRD STAGE (LATE EXPANDING): Declining death rate, falling birth rate. Growth continues but slows. (India is in this stage).
FOURTH STAGE (LOW STATIONARY): Low birth and death rates. Stationary population.
FIFTH STAGE (DECLINING): Birth rate is lower than death rate.

CONTRACEPTIVE METHODS:
INTRAUTERINE DEVICES (IUCD): Cu T 380A is approved for 10 years; Cu T 375 for 5 years. They work primarily by inducing a sterile, foreign-body inflammatory response in the endometrium that is highly spermicidal.
ORAL CONTRACEPTIVE PILLS (OCP): Combined pills (estrogen + progestin) act primarily by suppressing the LH surge, thereby inhibiting ovulation. Absolute contraindications include history of thromboembolism, suspected breast cancer, and active liver disease.

KEY POINTS
Replacement level fertility corresponds to a TFR of 2.1, which equates to an NRR of 1.
In India, there are approximately 150 to 160 eligible couples per 1000 population.
The Pearl Index is the standard measure of contraceptive efficacy.
Terminal methods (Vasectomy and Tubectomy) are intended for couples who have completed their desired family size.

FORMULAS AND CALCULATIONS
PEARL INDEX = (TOTAL ACCIDENTAL PREGNANCIES / TOTAL MONTHS OF EXPOSURE) X 1200
COUPLE PROTECTION RATE (CPR) = (NUMBER OF ELIGIBLE COUPLES PROTECTED BY CONTRACEPTION / TOTAL NUMBER OF ELIGIBLE COUPLES) X 100

MNEMONICS
ABSOLUTE CONTRAINDICATIONS FOR OCPS (T B L):
T - THROMBOEMBOLISM (HISTORY OF)
B - BREAST CANCER (SUSPECTED OR CONFIRMED)
L - LIVER DISEASE (ACTIVE)

title: Preventive Medicine in Obstetrics, Paediatrics and Geriatrics
content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
This chapter covers the vulnerable extremes of age and the critical periods of pregnancy and childbirth. It outlines antenatal care protocols, neonatal assessment, child growth monitoring, diarrheal disease management, and the unique health challenges of the aging population.
[Image of growth chart for children

DEFINITIONS
MATERNAL MORTALITY RATIO (MMR): The number of maternal deaths per 100,000 live births during a given time period.
INFANT MORTALITY RATE (IMR): The ratio of infant deaths (under 1 year of age) per 1000 live births in a given year.
LOW BIRTH WEIGHT (LBW): Birth weight less than 2500 grams (up to and including 2499 g), regardless of gestational age.

CORE CONCEPTS
MATERNAL HEALTH:
ANTENATAL CARE (ANC): Minimum of 4 visits recommended by MoHFW (1st: within 12 weeks; 2nd: 14 to 26 weeks; 3rd: 28 to 34 weeks; 4th: 36 weeks to term). WHO recommends 8 contacts.
HIGH-RISK PREGNANCY: Elderly primigravida (>30 yrs), short stature (<145 cm), severe anemia (Hb < 7 g/dL), pre-eclampsia.
PAEDIATRICS AND CHILD HEALTH:
KANGAROO MOTHER CARE (KMC): Skin-to-skin contact to prevent hypothermia in LBW infants; promotes exclusive breastfeeding.
APGAR SCORE: Assessed at 1 and 5 minutes post-birth. Normal is 7 to 10; moderately depressed is 4 to 6; severely depressed is 0 to 3.

GROWTH MONITORING: Utilizes the Road to Health chart. The direction of the curve is more important than the child's position at a single point. A flat or downward curve indicates growth faltering.

MANAGEMENT OF DIARRHOEAL DISEASES:
WHO LOW OSMOLARITY ORS: Total osmolarity is 245 mOsm/L. Sodium content is 75 mEq/L.
ZINC SUPPLEMENTATION: Reduces duration and severity of diarrhea. Dose: 10 mg/day (<6 months), 20 mg/day (>6 months) for 14 days.
GERIATRICS: Focuses on diseases of aging like cataracts, osteoarthritis, hypertension, and dementia. Aim is maintaining functional independence.

KEY POINTS
LBW is the single most important determinant of neonatal survival.
In the APGAR score, Heart Rate is the most important indicator of neonatal distress.
Neonatal mortality accounts for the largest proportion of infant mortality in India.

FORMULAS AND CALCULATIONS
MMR = (TOTAL NUMBER OF MATERNAL DEATHS / TOTAL NUMBER OF LIVE BIRTHS) X 100,000
IMR = (TOTAL NUMBER OF INFANT DEATHS UNDER 1 YEAR / TOTAL NUMBER OF LIVE BIRTHS) X 1,000

MNEMONICS
APGAR SCORE:
A - APPEARANCE (SKIN COLOR)
P - PULSE (HEART RATE)
G - GRIMACE (REFLEX IRRITABILITY)
A - ACTIVITY (MUSCLE TONE)
R - RESPIRATION (BREATHING EFFORT)

title: Health Care of the Community
content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
This chapter outlines the sprawling architecture of the healthcare delivery system in India. It highlights the historical committees that shaped the system, the population norms for health centers, and the vital roles played by grassroots workers in achieving universal health coverage.

DEFINITIONS
PRIMARY HEALTH CARE: Essential health care made universally accessible to individuals and acceptable to them, through their full participation and at a cost the community and country can afford.
FIRST REFERRAL UNIT (FRU): A facility equipped to provide round-the-clock services for emergency obstetric care, newborn care, and blood storage. Usually a Community Health Centre (CHC).

CORE CONCEPTS
MAJOR HEALTH COMMITTEES:
- BHORE COMMITTEE (1946): The Health Survey and Development Committee. Proposed the 3 million plan and heavily emphasized the integration of preventive and curative services at all administrative levels.
- MUDALIAR COMMITTEE (1962): The Health Survey and Planning Committee. Focused on consolidating the gains of the early five-year plans rather than massive expansion.
- CHADHA COMMITTEE (1963): Recommended that basic health workers should visit 10,000 population to conduct malaria vigilance and family planning duties.
- KARTAR SINGH COMMITTEE (1973): The Committee on Multipurpose Workers. Replaced separate unipurpose workers (like malaria or family planning workers) with unified Multipurpose Health Workers.
- SHRIVASTAV COMMITTEE (1975): Group on Medical Education and Support Manpower. Recommended the creation of the Village Health Guide scheme to bring healthcare to the doorstep.
RURAL HEALTH INFRASTRUCTURE NORMS:
- SUB-CENTRE: Serves a population of 5000 in plains and 3000 in hilly/tribal/difficult areas. Manned by a Health Worker Female (ANM) and Health Worker Male.
- PRIMARY HEALTH CENTRE (PHC): Serves a population of 30,000 in plains and 20,000 in hilly/tribal areas. It is the first port of call to a qualified medical officer.
- COMMUNITY HEALTH CENTRE (CHC): Serves a population of 1,20,000 in plains and 80,000 in hilly/tribal areas. Requires four core specialists: Surgeon, Physician, Obstetrician/Gynecologist, and Pediatrician.

GRASSROOTS WORKERS:
- ASHA (ACCREDITED SOCIAL HEALTH ACTIVIST): A female resident of the village, married/widowed/divorced, aged 25 to 45 years. Norm is generally 1 ASHA per 1000 population. Acts as the primary bridge between the community and the health system.
- ANGANWADI WORKER: Operates under the ICDS scheme, providing supplementary nutrition, non-formal preschool education, and health education to a population of 1000.

KEY POINTS
The Bhore Committee laid the foundational blueprint for India's post-independence health system.
Ayushman Arogya Mandirs (formerly Health and Wellness Centres) represent the upgrading of Sub-Centres and PHCs to deliver Comprehensive Primary Health Care (CPHC) beyond just maternal and child health.

FORMULAS AND CALCULATIONS
NOT APPLICABLE FOR THIS CHAPTER.

MNEMONICS
POPULATION NORMS FOR PLAINS (5, 30, 120):
SUB-CENTRE: 5 THOUSAND
PHC: 30 THOUSAND
CHC: 120 THOUSAND

title: Nutrition and Health
content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
This chapter focuses on macronutrients and micronutrients, their requirements, and the profound public health impact of malnutrition. It details specific deficiency disorders like Xerophthalmia, Nutritional Anemia, and Iodine Deficiency, along with diseases caused by food adulteration.


DEFINITIONS
NET PROTEIN UTILIZATION (NPU): The proportion of ingested protein that is retained in the body under standard conditions.
BIOLOGICAL VALUE (BV): The proportion of absorbed nitrogen that is retained in the body for maintenance and/or growth.
CONSUMPTION UNIT (CU): A standardized unit to calculate calorie requirements per family. An average adult male doing sedentary work is assigned a coefficient of 1.0 (equivalent to roughly 2400 Kcal/day).

CORE CONCEPTS
VITAMIN A DEFICIENCY (VAD): The leading cause of preventable childhood blindness. The WHO classification tracks the progression from early functional loss to severe structural damage.
WHO CLASSIFICATION OF XEROPHTHALMIA:
X1A: CONJUNCTIVAL XEROSIS
X1B: BITOT SPOTS (silvery, foamy triangular patches on the bulbar conjunctiva)
X2: CORNEAL XEROSIS
X3A: CORNEAL ULCERATION (<1/3 of cornea)
X3B: KERATOMALACIA (>= 1/3 of cornea)
XN: NIGHT BLINDNESS (Earliest functional symptom)

NUTRITIONAL ANEMIA: WHO cutoffs define anemia. For pregnant women and children (6 to 59 months), Hb < 11.0 g/dL. For non-pregnant women, Hb < 12.0 g/dL. For adult males, Hb < 13.0 g/dL.
IODINE DEFICIENCY DISORDERS (IDD): An area is endemic if goiter prevalence in school children is > 5 percent, or median urinary iodine is < 100 mcg/L. Iodized salt must contain 15 ppm iodine at the consumer level and 30 ppm at production.
FOOD TOXINS AND ADULTERATION:
LATHYRISM: Spastic paraplegia from consuming Lathyrus sativus (Khesari dhal). Toxic principle is BOAA (beta-oxalyl amino alanine).
EPIDEMIC DROPSY: Caused by mustard oil adulterated with Argemone mexicana. Toxic principle is Sanguinarine. Leads to severe bilateral pitting edema, glaucoma, and heart failure.
AFLATOXICOSIS: Toxin from Aspergillus flavus on improperly stored grains/groundnuts; strongly linked to hepatocellular carcinoma.

KEY POINTS
The NPU of a whole egg is assumed to be 100, serving as the reference standard.
Night blindness (XN) is the earliest symptom of VAD, while conjunctival xerosis (X1A) is the earliest clinical sign.
Vitamin A prophylaxis dose in India is 1 lakh IU at 9 months, then 2 lakh IU every 6 months until 5 years of age (total 9 doses).

FORMULAS AND CALCULATIONS
NET PROTEIN UTILIZATION = (RETAINED NITROGEN / INTAKE OF NITROGEN) X 100

MNEMONICS
FOOD TOXINS MATCHING (L-B, E-S):
LATHYRISM = BOAA
EPIDEMIC DROPSY = SANGUINARINE

title: Medicine and Social Sciences
content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
This chapter explores the sociological and behavioral dimensions of health. It highlights how cultural factors, family structures, and socio-economic status fundamentally influence health-seeking behavior and disease patterns in communities.

DEFINITIONS
SOCIOLOGY: The scientific study of human society, including social interactions, relationships, and institutions.
CULTURE: The acquired, learned, and shared behavior of a group of people, transmitted from generation to generation.
FAMILY: The primary social unit, consisting of individuals related by blood, marriage, or adoption.

CORE CONCEPTS
TYPES OF FAMILY: 
1. NUCLEAR FAMILY: Consists of married parents and their dependent children.
2. JOINT FAMILY: Traditional in India, consisting of three or more generations living together under one roof, sharing property and kitchen.
ROLE OF FAMILY IN HEALTH: The family is the basic unit of epidemiological study and the primary setting for health education and care. It shapes dietary habits, hygiene practices, and psychological well-being.
CULTURAL FACTORS IN HEALTH: Cultural beliefs significantly impact disease perception. Concepts like the 'evil eye' or specific dietary taboos during pregnancy are common in traditional societies.

KEY POINTS
The family provides critical social security and emotional support to vulnerable members (elderly, children, sick).
Acculturation refers to the cultural changes that occur when two distinct cultures come into continuous contact.
Medical social workers play a vital role as a bridge between the hospital and the community.

FORMULAS AND CALCULATIONS
NOT APPLICABLE FOR THIS CHAPTER.

MNEMONICS
FAMILY FUNCTIONS (E B S):
E - ECONOMIC SUPPORT
B - BIOLOGICAL REPRODUCTION
S - SOCIALIZATION

title: Tribal Health in India
content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
This chapter examines the unique health challenges faced by the tribal populations of India, who often reside in geographically isolated and socio-economically disadvantaged areas. It highlights the high burden of endemic diseases, malnutrition, and genetic disorders within these communities.

DEFINITIONS
SCHEDULED TRIBES: Indigenous communities formally recognized by the Constitution of India, characterized by primitive traits, distinctive culture, geographical isolation, shyness of contact with the community at large, and backwardness.
PARTICULARLY VULNERABLE TRIBAL GROUPS (PVTGs): Sub-groups among Scheduled Tribes characterized by a declining or stagnant population, pre-agricultural level of technology, extremely low literacy, and subsistence level of economy.

CORE CONCEPTS
HEALTH PROFILE OF TRIBES: Characterized by high maternal and infant mortality rates compared to the national average. 
COMMUNICABLE DISEASES: Extremely high burden of Vector-Borne Diseases, particularly P. falciparum Malaria. High prevalence of Tuberculosis and neglected tropical diseases like Yaws (historically) and Leprosy.
NUTRITIONAL DISORDERS: Severe protein-energy malnutrition and micronutrient deficiencies (iron, iodine, vitamin A) are rampant due to reliance on forest produce and subsistence farming.
GENETIC DISORDERS: High prevalence of Sickle Cell Anemia and G6PD deficiency, particularly in Central and Western Indian tribes (e.g., Bhils, Gonds), historically acting as a protective trait against severe malaria.

KEY POINTS
The Ministry of Tribal Affairs and the National Health Mission have dedicated sub-plans to improve tribal health infrastructure.
Healthcare delivery requires deep cultural sensitivity, often integrating modern medicine with traditional tribal healers to build trust.
Mobile Medical Units (MMUs) are frequently utilized to reach deep forest and hilly terrain where stationary health facilities are unviable.

FORMULAS AND CALCULATIONS
NOT APPLICABLE FOR THIS CHAPTER.

NOT APPLICABLE FOR THIS CHAPTER.

title: Sustainable Development Goals
content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
This chapter introduces the global framework established by the United Nations to achieve a better and more sustainable future for all by 2030. It bridges the gap between health and broader socio-economic and environmental determinants.
[Image of sustainable development goals

DEFINITIONS
SUSTAINABLE DEVELOPMENT: Development that meets the needs of the present without compromising the ability of future generations to meet their own needs.
SDGs (GLOBAL GOALS): A collection of 17 interlinked global goals designed to be a blueprint to achieve a better and more sustainable future for all, adopted in 2015 as the successor to the Millennium Development Goals (MDGs).

CORE CONCEPTS
THE 17 GOALS: While all are interconnected, specific goals directly target health and well-being.
SDG 1: No Poverty.
SDG 2: Zero Hunger (directly linked to nutrition).
SDG 3: GOOD HEALTH AND WELL-BEING. This is the primary health goal. It aims to ensure healthy lives and promote well-being for all at all ages.
SDG 4: Quality Education.
SDG 5: Gender Equality.
SDG 6: Clean Water and Sanitation.

KEY TARGETS UNDER SDG 3 (BY 2030):
- Reduce the global maternal mortality ratio to less than 70 per 100,000 live births.
- End preventable deaths of newborns and children under 5 years of age (aiming for neonatal mortality at least as low as 12 per 1000 live births and under-5 mortality at least as low as 25 per 1000 live births).
- End the epidemics of AIDS, tuberculosis, malaria, and neglected tropical diseases.
- Reduce by one-third premature mortality from non-communicable diseases.
- Achieve Universal Health Coverage (UHC).

KEY POINTS
Unlike MDGs which primarily targeted developing nations, SDGs are universally applicable to all countries.
Health is explicitly placed as a central outcome of sustainable development, heavily dependent on the success of other goals like sanitation, poverty reduction, and gender equality.

FORMULAS AND CALCULATIONS
NOT APPLICABLE FOR THIS CHAPTER.

MNEMONICS
SDG HEALTH TARGETS (M I N N):
M - MATERNAL mortality reduction (<70)
I - INFANT/child mortality reduction
N - NCD mortality reduction (by 1/3)
N - NEGLECTED tropical diseases and epidemics (end them)

title: Environment and Health
content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
This chapter emphasizes the paramount importance of a safe environment. It extensively covers the methods of large-scale water purification, criteria for safe drinking water, the mechanics of slow and rapid sand filters, and the public health implications of air pollution.

DEFINITIONS
SAFE WATER: Water that is free from pathogenic agents, free from harmful chemical substances, pleasant to the taste, and usable for domestic purposes.
POLLUTED WATER: Water that contains pathogenic organisms, undesirable foreign matter, or chemicals rendering it unfit for human consumption.
BIOCHEMICAL OXYGEN DEMAND (BOD): The amount of oxygen absorbed by a sample of sewage during a specified period (usually 5 days) at a specified temperature (usually 20 C) for the aerobic destruction of organic matter.

CORE CONCEPTS
WATER PURIFICATION ON A LARGE SCALE:
1. STORAGE: Provides time for natural settlement of physical impurities and reduction of bacterial count.
2. FILTRATION:
- SLOW SAND (BIOLOGICAL) FILTERS: The vital active layer is the Schmutzdecke (zoogleal layer) which removes 98 percent of bacteria. Cleaning is done by scraping off the top layer. Cannot treat highly turbid water.
- RAPID SAND (MECHANICAL) FILTERS: Requires chemical coagulation (usually alum) before filtration. Cleaning is done efficiently by backwashing.
3. DISINFECTION (CHLORINATION): Destroys pathogenic organisms. 
- Free residual chlorine must be at least 0.5 mg/L for a contact period of 1 hour to ensure safe drinking water.
- HORROCKS APPARATUS is used to find the exact chlorine demand of a water sample.
- ORTHOTOLIDINE TEST measures both free and combined chlorine. To measure them separately, the Orthotolidine-Arsenite (OTA) test is used.
AIR POLLUTION:
- INDICATORS: Sulfur dioxide (SO2) is traditionally considered the most reliable index of air pollution.
- HEALTH IMPACTS: Prolonged exposure to particulate matter (PM 2.5 and PM 10) directly correlates with exacerbations of COPD, asthma, and increased cardiovascular mortality.

KEY POINTS
Chlorine is highly effective against bacteria and viruses but has virtually no effect on the cysts of Entamoeba histolytica or the ova of helminths at normal doses.
The Schmutzdecke in a slow sand filter takes several days to form (the ripening period) and is biologically active, containing algae, plankton, diatoms, and bacteria.
PM 2.5 particles are small enough to penetrate deep into the alveolar regions of the lungs.

FORMULAS AND CALCULATIONS
CHLORINE DEMAND = CHLORINE APPLIED MINUS FREE RESIDUAL CHLORINE

MNEMONICS
WATER FILTRATION STAGES (S F D):
S - STORAGE
F - FILTRATION
D - DISINFECTION

title: Hospital Waste Management
content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
This chapter is critically important for infection control in healthcare settings. It details the statutory guidelines for the segregation, transportation, treatment, and disposal of biomedical waste generated in hospitals to prevent occupational hazards and environmental pollution.
[Image of biomedical waste segregation color coding

DEFINITIONS
BIOMEDICAL WASTE (BMW): Any waste generated during the diagnosis, treatment, or immunization of human beings or animals, or in research activities pertaining thereto.
SEGREGATION: The basic separation of different categories of waste generated at source to reduce the risks and cost of handling and disposal.
INCINERATION: A controlled combustion process where waste is completely oxidized and harmful microorganisms are destroyed at very high temperatures.

CORE CONCEPTS
COLOR CODING AND SEGREGATION RULES (LATEST GUIDELINES):
- YELLOW CATEGORY: Used for human anatomical waste, animal anatomical waste, soiled waste (blood-soaked cotton/bandages), expired or discarded medicines, chemical waste, and microbiology/biotechnology waste. Treatment is strictly by Incineration or deep burial.
- RED CATEGORY: Used for contaminated recyclable waste primarily made of plastics (e.g., IV tubes, catheters, syringes without needles, urine bags, gloves). Treatment is by Autoclaving or microwaving followed by shredding. Must never be incinerated to prevent toxic dioxin release.
- WHITE CATEGORY (TRANSLUCENT AND PUNCTURE-PROOF): Used for all sharps, including needles, scalpels, blades, and any contaminated metal object. Treatment is by dry heat sterilization or autoclaving followed by shredding.
- BLUE CATEGORY (CARDBOARD BOXES): Used for broken or discarded glassware and metallic body implants. Treatment is by disinfection and recycling.

SPILL MANAGEMENT: Blood spills should be contained and treated with 1 percent Sodium Hypochlorite solution with a contact time of at least 20 minutes before being wiped and discarded into the Yellow bin.

KEY POINTS
Segregation at the source is the single most important step in biomedical waste management.
Chlorinated plastics must never be incinerated as they release highly toxic dioxins and furans into the atmosphere.
Mercury spills must never be collected with bare hands or a vacuum cleaner; use stiff paper to scoop droplets into a sealable container.

FORMULAS AND CALCULATIONS
NOT APPLICABLE FOR THIS CHAPTER.

MNEMONICS
BMW CATEGORY DISPOSAL (Y I, R A):
Y - YELLOW means INCINERATE (Burn it to ash)
R - RED means AUTOCLAVE (Sterilize and recycle plastics)

title: Disaster Management
content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
This chapter outlines the public health response to natural and man-made disasters. It focuses on the crucial phases of the disaster cycle, effective triage systems, and strategies to prevent post-disaster disease outbreaks.


DEFINITIONS
DISASTER: Any occurrence that causes damage, ecological disruption, loss of human life, or deterioration of health and health services on a scale sufficient to warrant an extraordinary response from outside the affected community.
TRIAGE: The process of rapidly classifying the injured on the basis of the severity of their injuries and the likelihood of their survival with prompt medical intervention.
MITIGATION: Measures aimed at reducing the impact of disasters on a community.

CORE CONCEPTS
DISASTER CYCLE:
1. DISASTER IMPACT
2. DISASTER RESPONSE: Immediate actions taken after a disaster, including search and rescue, triage, and provision of basic needs.
3. REHABILITATION AND RECONSTRUCTION (RECOVERY)
4. MITIGATION
5. PREPAREDNESS: Proactive measures like early warning systems, stockpiling supplies, and conducting mock drills.

TRIAGE COLOR CODING:
- RED (IMMEDIATE): High priority, life-threatening injuries but treatable (e.g., tension pneumothorax).
- YELLOW (DELAYED): Medium priority, severe injuries but not immediately life-threatening.
- GREEN (AMBULATORY): Low priority, minor injuries (the 'walking wounded').
- BLACK (EXPECTANT/DEAD): No priority, injuries are incompatible with life or the patient is already dead.

DISEASE CONTROL: The immediate priority post-disaster is ensuring a safe water supply and adequate sanitation to prevent outbreaks of water-borne diseases like cholera and typhoid.

KEY POINTS
Routine mass immunization programs (e.g., for typhoid or cholera) are generally NOT recommended immediately following a disaster due to logistical challenges and delayed immunity.
Chlorination of water is the most critical public health intervention in the aftermath of floods.
Epidemiological surveillance must be rapidly established in relief camps to detect early signs of outbreaks.

FORMULAS AND CALCULATIONS
NOT APPLICABLE FOR THIS CHAPTER.

MNEMONICS
TRIAGE PRIORITIES (R Y G B):
R - RED (RUN to them first)
Y - YELLOW (Yield slightly)
G - GREEN (Go to them last)
B - BLACK (Beyond help)

title: Occupational Health
content:
OVERVIEW OF THE CHAPTER
This chapter explores the relationship between work and health. It details the physiological principles of ergonomics, the wide spectrum of occupational hazards (physical, chemical, biological, and dust-related), and the vital labor legislations designed to protect worker health and provide social security.

DEFINITIONS
ERGONOMICS: Derived from Ergon (work) and Nomos (law). It is the science of fitting the job to the worker, aiming to achieve mutual adjustment of man and his work to improve efficiency and well-being.
OCCUPATIONAL HAZARD: A risk accepted as a consequence of a particular occupation.
